focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The mosque courtyard still held the smell of wet foreheads and old jute when Halima came through the side gate. Men were shaking out the prayer mats and rolling them against the wall, slapping dust and damp from the reed weave. The noon sun had shifted far enough that half the yard lay under the mosque's shadow and half stayed hard with light. Women did not usually stand in the middle of that space unless somebody was in labor or dying. Today they stood under the low eaves and along the bamboo fence, saris drawn close under their chins, as if shade itself could count as modesty.

Halima had walked from the ghat without going home first. The mainland dust was still on the hem of her sari. Nura came beside her carrying the blue plastic folder Aminul had stuffed with photocopies and notes. The inside flap was still marked in pencil with the neighboring char's name Halima had finally given her at the lodge. She held the folder flat and high away from her hip as though the papers might bruise. The burn mark on her wrist flashed each time the folder shifted. Aminul followed two steps behind them in his pressed shirt, the collar already gone dark at the neck, his own file gripped away from his body to protect it from sweat. Mofiz was waiting on the low platform by the mosque wall in a white panjabi too clean for the char and sandals that had not touched mud all morning. The dyed edges of his beard had run a little in the humidity, leaving a faint tea-colored stain at his jaw.

The assembly had been his idea, which was one more reason Halima distrusted it. He had sent a boy to the ghat before their boat had even tied up, saying the men needed to hear what the mainland office had offered. The men, as if the women whose names would be pinched, stretched, erased, or...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he key from the counter. The brass was warm from another hand. "No. I decided it years ago, piece by piece, every time I put a child where paper could not kill them at once. Today only showed me the bill."

The boy at the desk looked up then, curious despite himself, and pushed his algebra notebook farther from the damp patch on the counter. He had ink on the side of his little finger and the soft mustache of a child hurrying toward manhood because wages required it. Halima wondered what household he belonged to on paper and what household actually waited for him after the shutters came down.

Aminul gave a small nod, whether in agreement or resignation she could not tell. "I've taken the room across the corridor," he said. "If there's word about an early bus, I'll knock. Tomorrow we talk again before the first bus. Rest if you can."

He left them to the stairs.

Upstairs, Halima set the market bag on the cot and kept her hand on the papers through the plastic. Possible, Aminul had said. She could already see the other room: stamped affidavit paper going damp under elbows, Jamila holding her face still while neighbors named the woman who had borne her and the aunt who had signed her into safety, Kashem listening while other people opened his life like a fish on a block, Rahima walking in from the western edge with dust whitening her cracked heels to say aloud what milk and sleepless nights had said thirty-five years before. Nura would be in that room too, hearing names still being withheld from her. More households might stay on the list. The char would pay by hauling its hidden stitching into public light.

"So that's the road," the girl said.

Halima kept her hand where it was. "That's the road," she said. "Now we see who will walk on it once they know what it costs."
